worth_it_explanation: Supply Path Optimization (SPO) is the process buyers run to choose the cleanest route to a publisher's inventory, and a publisher's reseller and made-for-advertising risk decides whether the publisher sits on the routes buyers keep or the routes they cut. This check is a review of the seller declarations and partner relationships a publisher already maintains across Magnite and the authorization files on its own domain, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time. The finding points to a concrete declaration or partner relationship the publisher can tighten, which can keep its inventory on the supply paths buyers prefer.
intro: SPO is the practice buyers use to reduce the number of intermediaries they buy through and to favor clean, declared paths to inventory. For a publisher selling through Magnite, an exchange that buyers reach through their SPO decisions, the routes a buyer keeps depend on how clearly the publisher's seller relationships are declared and how little made-for-advertising (MFA) or unauthorized reseller risk attaches to its inventory. This subcategory covers the publisher's side of that picture: whether redundant or low-value reseller paths to the same inventory are minimized so buyers see one clean path, and whether MFA and unauthorized reseller risk is monitored and reduced. These checks read the seller declarations and partner relationships rather than the price or yield mechanics those routes carry.

audit_checks.title: Redundant reseller paths to the same inventory are not minimized
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When the same impression is offered to a buyer through several reseller paths at once, the buyer's Supply Path Optimization can read the duplication as a sign of an unclean supply chain and route around the publisher rather than through it. Redundant paths also split the publisher's own demand across intermediaries, so neither the direct path nor the reseller paths show their full value, and the publisher cannot tell which route is earning its place.

educate.base:
A single impression can reach a buyer through more than one route. A publisher may sell the same inventory directly through Magnite and also through one or more resellers that resell into the same exchange, so the buyer sees the impression offered several times along different paths. Supply Path Optimization (SPO) is the process the buyer runs to pick the shortest, cleanest route to that inventory and to stop buying through the rest. When duplicate paths to the same inventory pile up, the buyer's SPO has to spend effort untangling them, and a chain that looks tangled is one a buyer can decide to route around entirely.

The cost of redundant paths is not only the impression of an unclean chain. Demand that could have flowed through one declared path splits across several, so the direct relationship and each reseller relationship each show a fraction of the value they could carry. That makes it harder for the publisher to see which route actually earns its keep, and it gives resellers that add little beyond an extra hop a place in the chain they may not deserve.

The declarations that describe these paths are public. A publisher's authorization file on its own domain, the ads.txt file, lists every seller account ID the publisher authorizes to sell its inventory, marked as a direct relationship or a reseller relationship. Magnite's sellers.json file lists the seller accounts it represents and whether each is the publisher itself or an intermediary. Read together, these files show how many declared paths lead to the same inventory and whether each one is a deliberate choice.

A few signs point to redundant reseller paths that have not been minimized:
  • The ads.txt file authorizes several reseller accounts that all resell the same inventory into the same exchange
  • Reseller relationships exist with partners the publisher cannot describe a current reason for, beyond historical setup
  • Reporting shows reseller paths carrying low volume or low value next to a direct path that could absorb the same demand
You may want to confirm that each reseller path to a given slice of inventory earns its place rather than duplicating a cleaner route. One approach is to list every declared path to the same inventory, then retire the reseller relationships that add an extra hop without adding demand.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, reseller relationships often accumulate over years as the publisher tries different monetization partners and leaves the older ones authorized. You may want to read the ads.txt file against current reporting and retire reseller authorizations for partners that no longer drive volume, so buyers see one clean path to the editorial inventory rather than several stale ones.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, the higher-intent product and category inventory is the inventory buyers most want a clean path to. You may want to make sure that inventory is sold through one declared direct path where possible, rather than duplicated across resellers, so the value of the commercial pages is concentrated on the route buyers prefer.

investigate.intro: Review the publisher's ads.txt file and Magnite's sellers.json entries to list every declared path to the same inventory, then read reporting to see which reseller paths carry meaningful demand and which duplicate a cleaner route.

ads_txt_file.label: Publisher ads.txt file
steps: Open the ads.txt file at the root of the publisher's domain (e.g., https://example.com/ads.txt).
List every Magnite or Rubicon Project entry and note the seller account ID and whether the relationship is marked 'DIRECT' or 'RESELLER'.
Group the entries by the inventory they cover and count how many reseller paths lead to the same inventory alongside the direct path.
Flag any reseller account the publisher cannot give a current reason for keeping authorized.

magnite_console.label: Magnite console and reporting
steps: Sign in to the Magnite console (the seller console for the publisher's exchange or SSP product) and open the seller or inventory reporting section.
Build a report broken out by the reseller or supply path carrying each slice of inventory, with impressions, revenue, and average price.
Compare the volume and value each reseller path carries against the direct path to the same inventory.
Record which reseller paths carry low or duplicate volume that the direct path could absorb.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner lists every declared path to each slice of inventory from the ads.txt file and Magnite's sellers.json, then records which reseller paths carry meaningful demand and which duplicate a cleaner route. A setup where each reseller path has a current reason and carries demand the direct path does not, passes. A setup where several reseller paths lead to the same inventory with low or duplicate volume and no stated reason is a fail recorded with the redundant paths and the inventory they cover.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the declared paths to each slice of inventory and the demand each reseller path carries above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} authorizes several reseller paths to the same inventory without a clear reason for each, so buyers see duplicate routes to one impression. Supply Path Optimization (SPO) leads buyers to favor the cleanest route to inventory and to route around chains that look tangled, and redundant reseller paths also split demand so no single route shows its full value. {{voice_recommend}} listing every declared path to each slice of inventory from the ads.txt file and Magnite's sellers.json, then retiring the reseller relationships that add an extra hop without adding demand. After the cleanup, recheck the ads.txt file and reporting to confirm each remaining path earns its place.

audit_checks.title: Made-for-advertising and unauthorized reseller risk is not monitored
audit_checks.impact_rationale: If a publisher's inventory becomes associated with made-for-advertising signals or its seller account IDs are declared by domains the publisher does not own, buyers can blocklist the inventory across their Supply Path Optimization and the revenue loss spreads well beyond the offending impressions. Unauthorized reseller declarations also expose the publisher to spoofing, where another party sells under the publisher's identity, which is a supply-chain fraud risk that erodes buyer trust at scale.

educate.base:
Made-for-advertising (MFA) describes sites built to win programmatic scale and churn out impressions while giving the visitor little of value, marked by heavy ad clutter, fast-refreshing slots, and thin or reworked content. Buyers increasingly screen MFA inventory out of their buying, and they do it through Supply Path Optimization (SPO), the process they use to favor clean supply and cut paths that carry risk. A legitimate publisher can still be caught by this in two ways. Its own inventory can pick up MFA-like signals through aggressive ad density or refresh, and its seller identity can be borrowed by other domains declaring its account IDs without authorization.

The MFA risk is a signal risk. Buyers read OpenRTB signals and on-page behavior to judge whether inventory looks made for advertising, and the tells are mechanical: how many ad slots load, how fast they refresh, how long the page takes to render, and whether the content reads as original or reworked. A publisher that runs a heavy refresh cadence or stacks slots to lift impression counts can drift toward those signals even with genuine content behind them, and once a buyer's SPO flags the inventory the loss is not limited to the flagged impressions.

The unauthorized-reseller risk is an identity risk. A publisher's seller account ID is meant to appear in the ads.txt files of domains the publisher actually owns or manages. When that same account ID shows up in the ads.txt file of a domain the publisher does not control, another party may be selling under the publisher's identity, which is the spoofing pattern these declarations exist to stop. The ownership variables in the ads.txt 1.1 standard, OWNERDOMAIN and MANAGERDOMAIN, let a publisher declare which domain owns and which manages its inventory, which narrows the room for an unauthorized seller to claim it.

A few signs point to MFA or unauthorized-reseller risk going unmonitored:
  • No regular check compares ad density, refresh cadence, and page load against the thresholds buyers use to flag MFA inventory
  • The publisher does not periodically scan for its own seller account IDs appearing in ads.txt files on domains it does not own
  • The ads.txt file does not use OWNERDOMAIN and MANAGERDOMAIN to declare ownership, leaving the publisher's identity easier to claim
You may want to confirm that both the MFA signals on the publisher's own pages and the unauthorized use of its seller identity are watched on a schedule. One approach is to set a periodic review of ad density and refresh against buyer MFA thresholds, paired with a scan for the publisher's account IDs on domains it does not control.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, the pressure to lift impression counts can push ad density and refresh cadence toward the levels buyers flag as made-for-advertising, even when the editorial content is genuine. You may want to review slot count, refresh timing, and page load against buyer MFA thresholds on a schedule, so the inventory keeps its clean reputation rather than drifting into a category buyers screen out.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, the product and category inventory is valuable to buyers and therefore a more tempting target for spoofing, where another domain declares the publisher's seller account IDs. You may want to scan regularly for the publisher's account IDs appearing on domains it does not own, and declare ownership with OWNERDOMAIN and MANAGERDOMAIN, so the commercial inventory is harder to sell under a borrowed identity.

learn_more.source_title: Using OpenRTB Signals to Identify Made for Advertising

investigate.intro: Review whether the publisher monitors its own pages for made-for-advertising signals and scans for unauthorized use of its seller account IDs, and confirm the ads.txt file declares ownership.

ads_txt_file.label: Publisher ads.txt file
steps: Open the ads.txt file at the root of the publisher's domain and confirm whether it includes the OWNERDOMAIN and MANAGERDOMAIN variables that declare ownership under the ads.txt 1.1 standard.
List the publisher's Magnite and Rubicon Project seller account IDs from the file.
Search the web for those account IDs to find other domains' ads.txt files that declare them, and flag any domain the publisher does not own or manage.
Record whether a scan for unauthorized declarations runs on a schedule or has never been done.

page_inspection.label: On-page MFA signal inspection
steps: Load a sample of the publisher's pages and count the ad slots, noting how many load above the fold and how the slots refresh.
Measure how long the page takes to render and how often ad slots refresh against the thresholds buyers use to flag made-for-advertising inventory.
Read a sample of the content for originality, author attribution, and depth rather than reworked or thin pages.
Record whether ad density, refresh cadence, and content quality sit within ranges buyers treat as clean, or whether any cross into MFA territory.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether the publisher monitors ad density, refresh cadence, and page load against buyer made-for-advertising thresholds, whether it scans for its seller account IDs on domains it does not own, and whether the ads.txt file declares ownership with OWNERDOMAIN and MANAGERDOMAIN. A setup that watches both the MFA signals and the unauthorized-reseller risk on a schedule and declares ownership, passes. A setup where neither is monitored and ownership is not declared is a fail recorded with the gaps found.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether MFA signals and unauthorized seller-ID use are monitored and whether ownership is declared in ads.txt above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} does not monitor its inventory for made-for-advertising (MFA) signals or scan for unauthorized use of its seller account IDs, and its ads.txt file does not declare ownership. Buyers screen MFA inventory out through Supply Path Optimization (SPO), and they treat seller IDs declared by domains a publisher does not own as a spoofing risk, so both gaps can cost demand well beyond the impressions involved. {{voice_recommend}} setting a periodic review of ad density, refresh cadence, and page load against buyer MFA thresholds, a regular scan for the publisher's account IDs on domains it does not control, and the OWNERDOMAIN and MANAGERDOMAIN declarations in the ads.txt file. After those are in place, recheck on the next cycle to confirm the signals stay clean and the identity stays the publisher's own.
